content.full_text:
George G. Logan

ATTORNEY AT LAW

2669 ALABAMA AVENUE
ATWATER, CALIFORNIA 95301
TEL. (209) 357-1431
FAX (209) 357-0940

June 24, 1992

California Fair Political Practices Commission
Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
Blanca M. Breeze, Counsel, Legal Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Request for Advice

Dear Mr. Hallabrin/Ms. Breeze:

The Berrenda Mesa Water District (District) is a California
Water District formed pursuant to Division 13 of the California
Water Code. (All references are to the Water Code.) It is a
landowner voting district with each landowner having one vote for
each dollar's worth of assessed valuation of land to which he holds
title (Section 35003). The District Assessor assesses all land
equally for District revenue collection purposes, resulting in
voting being directly proportional to land acreage. The District
was formed in 1963 and has been in active operation since 1968.

The District's sole source of water is a contractual supply
from the California State Water Project. Due to droughts and failure
of the State to complete certain portions of the Project, the State Water
Project has been periodically unable to supply its contractual
commitments. It appears the State Project will never be fully completed
and thus there will be a perpetual over-subscription for the water supply
contracted for delivery by the Project.

The District contains virtually no residential properties.
Only one landowner family lives in the District and the balance of
the residents are farm employees who live in farmer owned housing.
The District is comprised of approximately 55,000 acres which is
owned by approximately 60 different entities.

The District raises its revenue by virtue of ad valorem
assessments, water tolls and standby charges. The standby charges
are pursuant to section 35470 and are imposed on the basis of water
availability whether the water is used or not.

California Fair Political Practices Commission
June 24, 1992
Page 2


     The District is governed by a five member Board of Directors
elected by landowners. Two members of the board of directors are
employees of a corporation which owns over 17,000 acres in the
District; two other members are employees of another corporation
which owns over 12,000 acres in the District; and the fifth member
of the board is employed by a company which owns over 3,000 acres
in the District. The balance of the land is comprised of holdings
ranging from 2 to 3600 acres. All of the operating farms are large
(300 acres or more) and the smaller, unfarmed parcels are generally
not in the standby charge area.

     Due to the rising cost of water and power in the District,
annual crops have ceased to be planted and only permanent crops
(trees and vines) are currently economic to farm.

     The District's contractual water entitlement, if available, is
more than is necessary to irrigate existing crops but because of
the above stated conditions, the District's apportioned share of
the State Water Entitlement Supply in the last two years has been
less than was needed to properly irrigate the landowners' permanent
plantings.

     Some owners' lands are planted virtually 100% to permanent
crops; some owners lands are 100% open land; and some own a
combination of lands planted to permanent crops and open lands.

     The owners of the non-planted lands have, in recent years been
at odds with the owners of planted lands over issues such as the
imposition of the standby charge, the construction of groundwater
storage projects, the potential sale of the District's excess
contractual water entitlement and the foreclosure of properties
delinquent in payment of District taxes and standby charges.

     On the standby issue, some owners of the unplanted lands claim
that it is economically impractical to plant new trees and vines in
the District and want to eliminate the acreage standby charge and
to correspondingly increase the rate of the water tolls per acre
foot of water delivered. The standby charges are incorporated in
the District's by-laws and can only be changed by an election or by
a 4/5ths vote of the board, with approval of the Kern County Board
of Supervisors (Section 35305). Some owners of the planted lands
assert that the revenue program has been in effect since the
formation of the District, that standby charges encourage
development thus adding to the financial stability of the District,
that the program was in existence when they purchased their
properties, and they object to any "wholesale" change of that
program at this time.

California Fair Political Practices Commission
June 24, 1992
Page 3


     On the groundwater storage projects issue, some owners of
unplanted lands feel that these Projects are only for the benefit
of the planted lands and object to the District incurring further
obligations which are in turn a obligation on all District lands.

     On the water sale issue, some owners of unplanted lands wish
the District to permanently sell its excess contractual entitlement
to an outside entity and distribute a portion of the proceeds to
owners who wish to quit farming and permanently fallow their lands.
(Water Code Section 35425 permits the sale of "surplus water not
then necessary for use within the district".)  Some owners of the
planted lands assert that the State water supply has proven to be
unreliable and wish to maintain the excess entitlement to assure an
adequate supply in water short years.  (The District currently
contracts with its parent agency, the Kern County Water Agency for
the sale of any excess water allocations, within the county, on a
year to year basis.)

     On the foreclosure issue, several thousand acres have been
foreclosed by the District due to the failure of owners to pay
standby charges. Several thousand more acres are in default and on
the verge of being foreclosed.  Some owners of the delinquent lands
oppose foreclosure because the debt was incurred due to the standby
charges which they consider to be unfair.  Some owners of the
planted lands assert that foreclosure is necessary to enforce the
credibility of the standby charges and to protect the financial
integrity of the District and its bond holders.

     All of these issues are inter-related and have sub-issues and
other related issues and thus at many, if not most board meetings,
one or more issues arise which put these factions at odds.  The
board of directors appears to be currently split 3-2 on each of
these issues.

     There are obviously many millions of dollars at stake on both
sides and the board wishes your advice on whether they must abstain
from voting on these issues.  I have in the past considered that
the very nature of the District wherein a board member must be a
landowner (Section 34700), puts each member in a continued
"conflict of interest" and that the concept underlying Division 13
is that landowners controlling powers should be in direct
proportion to the extent of their economic interests. (Sec. 35003).
We have considered these issues to be those which affect the public
generally since the vast majority of the public are absentee
landowners with a common profit motive involved.

     I have considered your Advice Letter No. A-92-077 wherein you
recommend a "lot" method to requalify water district directors but

California Fair Political Practices Commission
June 24, 1992
Page 4


it appears that this method would bog this District down into chaos
because a different faction could be chosen on inter-related issues
resulting in inconsistent votes.  Also, if a permanent three
members were chosen by lot, it could result in the minority
controlling the affairs of the District for the remainder of their
terms which would be inconsistent with the concept of landowner-
value control.  Also, if a decision was made on one of these
issues, it could be irrevocable and would adversely affect the
majority in perpetuity.

Please give me your advice on whether these issues can be
considered to affect the public generally under Regulation §18703 or
whether the directors on either or both sides of these issues must
disqualify and requalify themselves.  Additionally, if they must
disqualify themselves and a quorum is lacking, can they all
requalify as opposed to using the "lot" method?

Please call me if you need any further information on the
matter.

Very truly yours,

GEORGE G. LOGAN
Attorney at Law

GGL:elz

cc:  District

George G. Logan

ATTORNEY AT LAW

2669 ALABAMA AVENUE
ATWATER, CALIFORNIA 95301
TEL. (209) 357-1431
FAX (209) 357-0940

September 9, 1992

Fair Political Practices Commission
Scott Hallabrin, Counsel, Legal Division
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95840

Re: Berrenda Mesa Water District
Request for Advice

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

This letter is written as a supplement to my Request for Advice dated June 24, 1992.

I urge upon you, the proposition that the normal rules of disqualification resulting from financial interests should not apply to landowner-voter districts in that the very nature of the enabling acts of such district contemplate that those with the largest financial interests should control the affairs of the district.

The second proposition I urge you to endorse is that the rule of necessity requires voting by all board members of landowner-voter districts in the event that a majority of the members of the board have a particular financial interest which disqualifies them from voting on certain issues.

1. FINANCIAL INTERESTS IN ISSUES AFFECTING ALL BOARD MEMBERS SHOULD NOT RESULT IN THE DISQUALIFICATION OF ANY BOARD MEMBERS

In my previous letter dated June 24, 1992, I set forth the various statutes pertaining to California Water Districts. There are similar provisions in the other landowner-voter district enabling acts and I will not restate them here. Suffice it to say that such acts specifically contemplate that the larger the financial interest held by a landowner, the more political clout he/she is granted under the voting system provided in

Fair Political Practices Commission
September 9, 1992
Page 2


such acts. For your Commission to rule that the very
interest which empowers control to the landowner under
the act also disqualifies the landowner from voting,
would eviscerate the legal foundation of the act and
could result in leaderless chaos.

It has been argued that the landowner voting scheme
is illogical and even illegal as being in violation of
the Equal Protection clause of the United States
Constitution. Several cases, however, have upheld this
method of landowner control of special districts and have
explained the necessity and wisdom of such control in the
process.

In the case of Salyer Land Co. v. Tulare Water
District, 410 U.S. 719, 35 L.Ed. 659, 93 S.Ct. 1224, the
court upheld the landowner voting scheme for California
Water Storage Districts and in so doing, spoke as
follows:

"...The California Legislature could quite
reasonably have concluded that the number of
landowners and owners of sufficient amounts of
acreage whose consent was necessary to
organize the district would not have subjected
their land to the lien of its possibly very
substantial assessments unless they had a
dominant voice in its control. Since the
subjection of the owners' lands to such liens
was the basis by which the district was to
obtain financing, the proposed district had as
a practical matter to attract landowner
support. Nor, since assessments against
landowners were to be the sole means by which
the expenses of the district were to be paid,
could it be said to be unfair or inequitable
to repose the franchise in landowners but not
residents. Landowners as a class were to bear
the entire burden of the district's costs, and
the State could rationally conclude that they,
to the exclusion of residents, should be
charged with responsibility for its operation.
We conclude, therefore, that nothing in the
Equal Protection Clause precluded California
from limiting the voting for directors of
appellee district by totally excluding those
who merely reside within the district."
(Emphasis added)

Fair Political Practices Commission
September 9, 1992
Page 3


     In further justifying the refusal to allow non-
owners to vote, the court said:

     "Finally, we note that California has not
left "the lessee without remedy for his
disenfranchised state.  Sections 41002 and
41005 of the California Water Code provide for
voting in the general election by proxy.  To
the extent that a lessee entering into a lease
of substantial duration, thereby likening his
status more to that of a landowner, feels that
the right to vote in the election of directors
of the district is of sufficient import to
him, he may bargain for that right at the time
he negotiates his lease.  And the longer the
term of the lease, and the more the interest
of the lessee becomes akin to that of the
landowner, presumably the more willing the
lessor will be to assign his right.  Just as
the lessee may by contract be required to
reimburse the lessor for the district
assessments so he may by contract acquire the
right to vote for district directors."

     Under these circumstances, the exclusion
of lessees from voting in general elections
for the directors of the district does not
violate the Equal Protection Clause."

     The later case of Ball v. James 451 U.S. 1811,
101 S.Ct 356, involved an Arizona Agricultural and Power
District. In upholding landowner voter control based on
size of holdings, the court said:

     "...We conclude that the voting scheme
for the District is constitutional because it
bears a reasonable relationship to its
statutory objectives. Here, according to the
stipulation of the parties, the subscriptions
of land which made the Association and then
the District possible might well have never
occurred had not the subscribing landowners
been assured a special voice in the conduct of
the District's business."

     "...Moreover, Arizona could rationally
make the weight of their vote dependent upon
the number of acres they own, since that

Fair Political Practices Commission
September 9, 1992
Page 4


number reasonably reflects the relative risks
they incurred as landowners and the
distribution of the benefits and the burdens
of the District's water operations."

In summary, it is clear that the California
Legislature and the United States Supreme Court both
heartily endorse the concept of landowner control based
upon the size of holdings. For this commission to rule
that the very qualifications which grant control also
disqualifies one from voting would be indeed incongruous.

2.  DISQUALIFICATION OF A MAJORITY SHOULD RESULT
    IN RE-QUALIFICATION OF ALL

It is not our position that no land-owner voter
should ever be disqualified. For example, if a project
were built to benefit only a portion of the District, the
directors owning land in that area should be disqualified
from voting. If, however, a majority were so
disqualified, it is our position that the entire board
should be re-qualified under the "rule of necessity" as
opposed to requalification of a mere majority under the
"lot" method suggested by your predecessors.

In the case of Olson v. Cory (1980), 27 Cal.3d 532,
the California Supreme Court was confronted with a case
which affected the salaries of every member of the court.
Instead of requalifying a quorum by lot, the court stated
that the whole court was qualified and spoke as follows:

"The rule of necessity provides that a
judge is not disqualified from adjudicating a
cause because of a personal financial interest
if there is no other judge or court available
to hear and resolve the cause."

In 1 Am Jur 2d Administrative Law §66, the rule is
stated as follows:

"There is an exception, based upon
necessity, to the rule of disqualification of
an administrative officer. Disqualification
will not be permitted to destroy the only
tribunal with power in the premises. An
officer, otherwise disqualified, may still
act, if his failure to act would necessarily
result in a failure of justice.

Fair Political Practices Commission
September 9, 1992
Page 5


"Thus where the statute clearly requires a
hearing before a designated administrative
officer, and no other officer can hold the
hearing, then the language of the statute may
not be disregarded nor the legislative intent
defeated by holding that the officer is
disqualified."

The case of Atkins v. United States 556 F2d 1028
(1977) involved the contention by a judge that judges
were not subject to income tax increases during their
term of office.

In holding that the entire appellate panel was
qualified to vote, the court spoke as follows:

"...If the judges of this court are
disqualified because of their indirect
interest in the subject matter in controversy
or because of any other interest that could be
substantially affected by the outcome of the
proceedings, all of the other judges of the
United States are similarly disqualified.
There is no federal judge who would be
available to try the cases. Situations of
this kind caused a doctrine known as the rule
of necessity to be developed in the law."

"The rule of necessity was a part of the
English common law and has been traced back to
1430 and the Year Books. Dimes v. Grand
Junction Canal Co., 10 Eng. Rep. 301, 313
(1852). See also, The Independence of the
Judiciary, 34 Can.B.Rev. 769, 790 (1956); and
Frank, Disqualification of Judges, 56 Yale
L.J. 605, 609-10 (1947). The rule, simply
stated, means that a judge is not disqualified
to try a case because of his personal interest
in the matter at issue if there is no other
judge available to hear and decide the case."

"Another statute, 28 U.S.C. §455,
provides that a judge shall disqualify himself
in any case in which he has a substantial
interest or his impartiality might be
questioned. Ordinarily when a judge is named
as a defendant in a suit brought by a

Fair Political Practices Commission
September 9, 1992
Page 6


defendant before him in trial, such judge
should automatically disqualify himself.

However, necessity may obviate this rule
when virtually no judge would be available to
hear the suit because all federal judges are
co-defendants."

"With respect to disqualification in
civil actions where the trial judge to which
the case happens to be assigned is also a
defendant in the same action. 28 U.S. C.A.
§455 would require that the judge disqualify
himself. This Court notes, however, that
there is a maxim of law to the effect that
where all are disqualified, none are
disqualified. Evans v. Gore, 253 U.S. 245, 40
S.Ct. 550, 64 L.Ed. 887."

"...Nevertheless we realize that the
plaintiffs are entitled to have their cases
heard and decided by a court of the United
States, and under the law there is no other
court to which they could go. Should we
refuse to hear and decide their cases, the
doors of the courts would be closed to them.
This could amount to a denial of due process
under the 14th amendment to the Constitution."

The case of Brinkley v. Hassig 82 F2d 351 involved
the revocation of a doctor's license by a medical board
whose members had all been exposed to prejudicial pre-
hearing publicity. In holding that the board was not
disqualified. The court spoke as follows:

"From the very necessity of the case has
grown the rule that disqualification will not
permitted to destroy the only tribunal with
power in the premises. If the law provides
for a substitution of personnel on a board or
court, or if another tribunal exists to which
resort may be had, a disqualified member may
not act. But where no such provision is made,
the law cannot be nullified or the doors to
justice barred because of prejudice or
disqualification of a member of a court or an
administrative tribunal."

Fair Political Practices Commission
September 9, 1992
Page 7


     In summary, where a majority of the members of a
landowner-voter district are disqualified because of
particular interests in a particular project, the entire
board should be allowed to vote under the "rule of
necessity" endorsed by the above authorities.

     To hold otherwise would deprive the landowners of
the very control granted to them by the Legislature and
endorsed by the United States Supreme Court.

     It could also result in extremely unacceptable
circumstances.    For instance, as alluded to in my
previous letter, one set of directors could vote one
month to construct a multi-million dollar storage or
transmission facility and another set of directors could
vote the next month to sell a major portion of the
District's water entitlement, thus rendering the
improvements unnecessary or useless.

     It is respectfully requested that you grant a letter
of advice holding:

     1.    That in instances of financial interests
           affecting an entire landowner-voter district,
           the members of the board are not disqualified
           from voting; and

     2.    that in instances where a majority of a
           landowner-voter district board are
           disqualified due to a particular financial
           interest, the entire board is requalified
           under the "rule of necessity."


                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [SIGNATURE]
                                        GEORGE G. LOGAN
                                        Attorney at Law

GGL:elz

cc:  -District
     -Ernest Conant, Atty.
     -Association of California Water Agencies
      Attn: Connie Barber

embedding.first_500_words: George G. Logan ATTORNEY AT LAW 2669 ALABAMA AVENUE ATWATER, CALIFORNIA 95301 TEL. (209) 357-1431 FAX (209) 357-0940 June 24, 1992 California Fair Political Practices Commission Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel Blanca M. Breeze, Counsel, Legal Division 428 J Street, Suite 800 P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 Re: Request for Advice Dear Mr. Hallabrin/Ms. Breeze: The Berrenda Mesa Water District (District) is a California Water District formed pursuant to Division 13 of the California Water Code. (All references are to the Water Code.) It is a landowner voting district with each landowner having one vote for each dollar's worth of assessed valuation of land to which he holds title (Section 35003). The District Assessor assesses all land equally for District revenue collection purposes, resulting in voting being directly proportional to land acreage. The District was formed in 1963 and has been in active operation since 1968. The District's sole source of water is a contractual supply from the California State Water Project. Due to droughts and failure of the State to complete certain portions of the Project, the State Water Project has been periodically unable to supply its contractual commitments. It appears the State Project will never be fully completed and thus there will be a perpetual over-subscription for the water supply contracted for delivery by the Project. The District contains virtually no residential properties. Only one landowner family lives in the District and the balance of the residents are farm employees who live in farmer owned housing. The District is comprised of approximately 55,000 acres which is owned by approximately 60 different entities. The District raises its revenue by virtue of ad valorem assessments, water tolls and standby charges. The standby charges are pursuant to section 35470 and are imposed on the basis of water availability whether the water is used or not. California Fair Political Practices Commission June 24, 1992 Page 2 The District is governed by a five member Board of Directors elected by landowners. Two members of the board of directors are employees of a corporation which owns over 17,000 acres in the District; two other members are employees of another corporation which owns over 12,000 acres in the District; and the fifth member of the board is employed by a company which owns over 3,000 acres in the District. The balance of the land is comprised of holdings ranging from 2 to 3600 acres. All of the operating farms are large (300 acres or more) and the smaller, unfarmed parcels are generally not in the standby charge area. Due to the rising cost of water and power in the District, annual crops have ceased to be planted and only permanent crops (trees and vines) are currently economic to farm. The District's contractual water entitlement, if available, is more than is necessary to irrigate existing crops but because of the above stated conditions, the District's apportioned share of the State Water Entitlement Supply in the last two years has been less than was needed to
